data.title: Barrick's North America IPO Is The Catalyst I Was Waiting For
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Let me start by saying that I am reiterating Barrick Mining (B) with a Strong Buy. And this time, I am doing so but for a different reason. In my previous piece, I said that Barrick checked all my boxes. Well, I still believe so. The difference now is that the next leg of upside does not need to come from another Fourmile headline. Or from a fresh gold spike alone. B has now moved from talking about value creation to actively preparing for it. And I say this because B's board has decided to move forward with preparations for an IPO of Barrick's North American gold assets. The proposed NewCo is expected to be anchored by Nevada Gold Mines, Pueblo Viejo and Barrick's wholly owned Fourmile discovery. All this while Barrick keeps a controlling interest. In my opinion, this move changes the valuation story in a big way. This is because the market usually discounts complexity. And Barrick is finally giving investors a cleaner way to price its best neighborhood.

This is why I think my current thesis is even stronger today than it was in the prior article. Since later 2025, Barrick has been advancing the North America IPO plan. It has also resolved its dispute with Mali and also restarted the path forward with Loulo Gounkoto. I also note that B has formalized dividend framework that targets 50% of attributable FCFs. And this can be evidenced by the $0.42 dividend that it recently announced. As if that is not enough, the company has also repurchased $1.5 Bn worth of stock. This is after finishing the year with $6.7Bn in cash and a $2Bn in net cash position. In my view, this is a company with a strong balance sheet, a visible catalyst and enough cash generation to keep rewarding shareholders while the market catches up. I am saying this because mining investors do not get to eat today and still keep dessert for later. But for Barrick, it is one of the rare cases where that is happening.

Given that I discussed the business previously, I will jump straight into the growth drivers. Because that is where things get interesting.

Growth Drivers

The new growth angle here is quite straightforward. Barrick's North American assets are already big enough to stand on their own. And the market is being invited to look at them that way. In 2025, B's North America region produced 2.093 Moz of gold. It also generated $7.56 Bn in revenue and delivered $4.43Bn in attributable EBITDA. That region alone accounted for 64% of Barrick's attributable gold production. And this tells me that the region seems to be the center of the portfolio. Mark Hill, the CEO said that NewCo could be "in a league of its own." This quote is important because Barrick is not carving out a weak child to flatter the parent. It is packaging its best jurisdictional exposure with some of its best growth optionality. And that could narrow the discount investors have been applying to the broader group.

The other piece is execution. And here too I think the setup has improved. In 4Q, North America finished strongly. With Carlin production up 25% q/q, Pueblo Viejo on the other side under the leadership of Tim Cribbs as regional COO hit another record high. B's 2026 guidance still calls for 2.90 Moz to 3.25 Moz and between 190k to 220k Tonnes of copper. To me it means that the management is not presenting the IPO as a substitute for operations. The two are supposed to work together. And that matters because I believe corporate stories only rerate when the operating tape stays clean enough to support them. In B's case, the market now has a structural catalyst. But it also has better quarterly proof points from the exact assets expected to anchor that catalyst.

When I look at Barrick's growth grades, it reinforces my point. The company has its growth grade at B+. Its FWD Revenue growth rate stands at 24.49%, while the FWD EBITDA growth rate stands at 37%. Additionally, the FWD operating cash flow growth sits at 41.64%. Now I would say those are not sleepy old miner numbers. I mean, relative to the sector median, the gaps are huge across the income and cash flow lines. And this shows me that the rerating case is not resting on financial engineering alone. It also means that there is real growth underneath the hood.

One reason why I like Barrick is that shareholders are not being asked to wait empty-handed. I say this because Barrick generated $7.69Bn of operating cash flow and $3.87Bn of FCFs in 2025. It also returned a record $2.4Bn to shareholders. And like I said before, it repurchased stock and issued a new dividend policy. Remember, the $0.42 payout was one of the highest shareholder returns in company history. To me this means that even if the IPO takes time to fully translate into a rerating. Then Barrick's machine is now strong enough to keep paying investors while the value unlock develops.

I would also like to talk about the copper layer. I do not want to ignore it even if it is not the center of this article. Lumwana's Super pit expansion is tracking slightly ahead of schedule. The mine ended 2025 with positive FCFs despite self-funding $254Mn of expansion spend. And Barrick's 2028 outlook points to copper production of 225K to 285K tonnes. This is an increase from 220K tonnes in 2025. Ideally this gives Barrick an extra layer of medium-term growth that many gold-only peers simply do not have. At the same time, I am not baking Reko Diq into my thesis. And this is because B said that it would slow down development activity and extend its review period by 12 months because of security issues. In my view, Lumwana is the near-term copper level. Reko Diq is still valuable, but for now, I am putting it into the optionality bucket.

Valuation

First, Barricks scores a B Valuation grade. B has its FWD non-GAAP P/E at 11.69x while the sector median trades at 16.05x. This suggests that B is trading at a 27% discount relative to the sector median. Additionally, when I look at the FWD EV/EBITDA, I note that the company is trading at 5.23x while the sector median trades at 8.47x. This again implies a 38% discount. Lastly, its FWD non-GAAP PEG sits at 0.36x while the sector median sits at 8.47x. This suggests a 71% discount relative to the sector median. In the earlier piece, B's valuation stack sat at about 21.36x FWD non-GAAP P/E, 9.26x FWD EV/EBITDA and 0.46x FWD non-GAAP PEG. In my view, the company is now cleaner, richer in cash and more shareholder-friendly. And now it is even close to a structural rerating event. Yet the valuation numbers that matter the most to me show that the company is even cheaper than the last time I looked at it. That kind of disconnect is where I would pay close attention.

When I compare to peers, the discount is still hard to ignore. On FWD non-GAAP P/E numbers, Newmont (NEM) stands at 12.3x, while Agnico Eagle (AEM) sits at 15.13x and Kinross Gold (KGC) has its multiple at 10.21x. Next, on FWD EV/EBITDA NEM stands at 8.2x while AEM at 11.3x. And lastly KGC sits at 8x. I do not think Barrick deserves to be the cheapest in this group when it now combines net cash, a formal 50% FCFs payout framework, a North America value unlock catalyst and a political overhand after Mali. And lastly, on FWD Non-GAAP PEG, Barrick still leads. And that is just enough for me. Because it means growth this strong, priced this cheaply, rarely stays ignored forever.

Key Risks

The biggest risk that I see for B is the execution on the IPO itself. Barrick has been quite clear that there is no assurance the IPO will be pursued or completed on the timeline or terms currently contemplated. And this means that if the structure gets delayed, resized or priced in a way that it disappoints, then it means that the rerating could take longer than I expect. I am saying this because my thesis is tied to the market seeing B's North American assets more clearly. And if that window closes or gets pushed far, then it means investors may go back to valuing B as a broad miner with a conglomerate discount.

Final Thoughts

The bottom line is that I am still reiterating B as a Strong Buy for many of the same reasons as before. But the reason I am pressing the case is now different. I am not making Fourmile the main act again. Though it still matters. But now it works better for me as extra torque inside a cleaner North America rerating story. I say this because B has scale, asset quality, net cash, rising shareholder returns, stronger North American execution and a broad level move that can force the market to value its best assets more rationally. And honestly that is a strong combination. In my view, the market is still pricing B like a broad, messy miner. Yet the company's management is clearly trying to expose a premium franchise inside the portfolio. And that gap is where I see the upside.

data.title: J.B. Hunt: The Hunt For Further Upside Is Risky
data.description:
More than a year after my first analysis, J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. (JBHT) has already risen dramatically and given over 50% returns. I can’t blame investors considering JBHT’s solid fundamentals and strong market positioning amid market undercapacity. But now, we are seeing mounting headwinds, which may erode its value. Valuation reflects overpricing and downside risks. Technicals are still in an uptrend, but overbuying and early bearish divergence trend signals caution.

JBHT Q1 2026: A Solid Start

Macroeconomic conditions remain uncertain amid stubborn inflation, tariff wars, and geopolitical tensions. Freight and logistics companies are often the first ones to feel the combined impact of these aspects. Even an established business like J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. is not entirely shielded from these mounting headwinds. Yet, its well-diversified business model and strong network capacity allow it to take advantage of the situation. This was visible in its most recent performance.

In Q1 2026, its operating revenue amounted to $3.1B, up by 4.6% YoY from $2.9B. This was quite a big difference from my first analysis because revenues dropped by 4.8% YoY. I think this was impressive amid the softer market conditions, driven by weaker consumption and higher cost pressures in most businesses. I think one of the factors that help it is its strategic business diversification. It does not concentrate heavily on traditional truckload services like many companies in the industry. Instead, it caters to various market needs by integrating its own with outsourced platforms and acting as a middleman between shippers and third-party service providers.

Another factor is its pricing strategy. For instance, it lowered its revenue per load in the intermodal segment to attract more loads. In other segments like Integrated Capacity Solutions and Truckload, demand was really high, allowing it to raise prices. But more importantly, I think the skyrocketing oil prices also drove its growth since it led to higher fuel surcharges. I will explain this more later.

Meanwhile, the operating expenses increased in line with its increased volumes transported and higher oil prices. But it was able to control salaries and general and administrative expenses. The amount increased by only 3.8%, which was easily offset by revenue growth. Hence, the operating margin reached 6.8% versus 6.1% YoY.

Middle East Tension: Ups And Downs

As a business in the freight and logistics industry, JBHT is exposed to risks associated with stubborn inflation. Currently, it stays above the 2.0% target, so you can say that the Fed still can't fully keep it under control. This may be amplified by tariff wars due to their tendency to raise domestic prices. With that, production and consumption may be affected. JBHT may also experience weaker volume if inflation intensifies.

It must also watch out for the potential impact of the Middle East tension. It has already sent oil prices above $100 per barrel. Although prices have already slowed down after the ceasefire agreement, their upside impact on fuel prices may still materialize. With that, JBHT may incur higher fuel expenses. Aside from that, higher fuel prices will affect various industries like shipping, logistics, manufacturing, and industrial. Because these are asset-heavy industries and directly affect the supply chain and production of other industries, inflation may be exacerbated. As a result, JBHT’s pricing flexibility may weaken. It may incur more cost pressures since it will have to raise wages and drivers’ insurance, so employees and drivers can keep up with the rising prices.

On a lighter note, I think the tension can have some positive spillovers into the company. Many businesses may respond by immediately increasing their shipments or rushing into inventory restocking before the prices of raw materials and fuel surcharges rise further. I believe this is a factor that could have driven volume growth in Q1 2026, particularly in March. The trend may continue this Q2 2026 as businesses try to move as fast as they can before oil supply shocks and supply chain disruptions happen. After all, they will not only affect their production but also raise their operating expenses further.

Mitigants/Drivers

The Middle East tension appears to be a double-edged sword for JBHT. So, it is important to consider these aspects, which may mitigate risks or drive growth.

One aspect is its well-diversified business portfolio. It operates across multiple segments, which allows it to cater to different and specific needs of businesses. An example is its dedicated contract services, which provides customized fleets and drivers for its specific clients like Walmart (WMT). This segment also includes routing, scheduling of loads, and customized supply chain design. Basically, this model is an outsourced private trucking fleet. Another is its Brokerage Services, which is one of its solid growth drivers. JBHT will act as a middleman between shippers and third-party carriers by matching loads with carriers. This model is asset-light and low-cost since it doesn’t involve its trucks and drivers. JBHT serves as an Uber-like marketplace for freight and logistics. With that, it maintains its huge domestic market coverage.

Another is its solid operations and network capacity. Amid the intensifying market volatility, JBHT may derive potential opportunities. Market undercapacity and driver shortages are still a challenge, leading to some capacity constraints. This allows companies with strong capacity networks to raise freight rates. After all, they can capture the demand or clients of smaller peers struggling with weak market conditions. If the impact of the skyrocketing oil prices intensifies, more trucking companies may shut down, like what happened in 2025. So, market capacity may decrease more than demand, allowing freight rates to remain high or resilient.

Lastly, JBHT maintains decent liquidity. Cash and cash equivalents may be low at only $4.6M. It still has receivables of $1.3B to support its liquidity. This should not be surprising since it maintains adequate operating cash inflows to cover capex. JBHT complements it with prudent debt management through timely issuances and repayments. And as you can see, its maturity profile improves since no amount will mature within the year. Also, the total borrowings have already decreased by about $166M. This is in line with the net debt/EBITDA of 0.8x, which means that it only needs 0.8 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. With its low leverage, this proves that it still has enough capital buffer to sustain its capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is $1.80, so the growth rate is 10.18%. I set its risk premium at 6.0% to be conservative since the market standard is 5.0%. Lastly, I estimated its beta at 1.12, which means that JBHT is still more volatile than the S&P 500. With all this information, I derived a TP or target price, of $191.49. This justifies the recent rally. However, JBHT appears to have already increased more than necessary.

I also made updates on its price ratios, which also show that the stock is overpriced. P/E, for instance, shows that the stock trades at 34.76x, which can be considered high. It is also much higher than the five-year average of 25.85x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP or target price, will be $166.75. On top of that, its current EPS growth relative to P/E is not explosive at 10%. With that, its PEG is 3.5x (34.76/10), which means that the stock trades 3.5x times its earnings growth. This proves that the stock is overvalued.

Technicals

JBHT’s technicals are still generally bullish, although we are seeing some weaknesses. The price is still moving above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. However, it is important to note that the most recent high of $229 yesterday is still lower than the $236 resistance. So, it is essential that JBHT break above it. Note that the most recent low of $195 broke below the first key support level of $198. If the price decreases again and breaks below the most recent low, a bearish reversal is possible. It is also worth noting that the 50-day SMA has flattened, showing that market sentiments are still optimistic but with some caution recently.

Its MACD and RSI also warrant some caution. Look at the increasing price high from February 3 to March 4 versus the decreasing MACD and RSI high on the same dates. This may tell us that fewer buyers entered when the stock became more expensive. While buying volume still outpaces selling volume, momentum is not as strong as in previous months. Additionally, the stock has become oversold recently, which may prompt profit-taking and a dip. Lastly, the histogram is still within positive territory but decreasing. This implies that selling volume may catch up with buying volume, considering the notable price decrease from the opening to closing yesterday.

Conclusion

J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc., is a solid company with well-diversified services and solutions to cover a vast market. This is supported by its robust fundamentals, which ensure its sustainability and dividend longevity. However, valuation is already a bit too high for a buy position. Technicals adhere to it as early bearish divergence signals emerge, fueled by the recent overbuying. I’m reiterating my Hold rating on JBHT stock.

data.title: BOXX: A Creative Way To Buy A Synthetic T-Bill
data.description:
The Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF (BOXX) is an actively managed ETF that aims to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, equal or exceed the price and yield performance of an investment that tracks the 1-3 month sector of the United States Treasury Bill market while offering primarily capital gain tax treatment on any gains or losses.

The fund attempts to do this through long- and short-dated SPX options that are structured as box spreads (which synthetically create a zero-coupon bond). I am initiating BOXX as a buy on Seeking Alpha. The pre-tax return tracks T-bills closely, and the after-tax return for a high-bracket taxable investor is meaningfully better than SGOV, which is the entire point of holding it. With my rates view of one Fed cut at most in 2026, the front-end yield environment is favorable, and the structural after-tax pickup compounds at approx. 60-70 bps annually for the investors that should be holding this product. However, Section 1258 tax overhang is real and is what keeps this from being a strong buy.

What is a Box(x) Spread?

The box spread strategy is what BOXX revolves around. A box spread is a four-leg options combination that synthetically replicates a zero-coupon bond. The structure uses four SPX (not SPY) options, all with the same expiration date, at two different strike prices. A box spread uses a long call and a short put at a lower strike (the synthetic long), combined with a short call and a long put at a higher strike (the synthetic short). The synthetic long and the synthetic short cancel each other out, which means the combined position has zero exposure to where the S&P 500 actually trades. What remains is a guaranteed payoff at expiration equal to the difference between the two strike prices.

As an example, the SPX is at 7,000, and an investor constructs a 60-day box spread using the 6,500 and 7,500 strikes. The four legs are as follows: buy the 6,500 call, write the 6,500 put, write the 7,500 call, buy the 7,500 put. The locked-in payoff at expiration is $1,000 per box (the 7,500 - 6,500 strike, times 100 for standard contract size, which is $100,000 per box). To enter the position, the buyer pays a net premium of approx. $993.50 ($99,350 notional) today. Sixty days later at expiration, the box settles for exactly $1,000 ($100,000 notional) regardless of where SPX prints. The buyer earned $6,500 on $99,350 of capital deployed for 60 days, which annualizes to approx. 3.94%. That is the implied lending rate. The writer on the other side of the trade receives approximately $993.50 today and pays back $1,000 in 60 days, owing the same implied rate as the cost of borrowing. (For reference, overnight SOFR is approx. 3.66% as of mid-April 2026, so the current implied lending rate on SPX box spreads of approx. 3.94% sits at approx. SOFR + 28 bps.)

This type of structure is a collateralized loan between two options market participants, intermediated by the Options Clearing Corporation, with no credit risk because the OCC guarantees settlement on all listed options. There is no counterparty exposure, no underlying directional risk, no early exercise risk if European-style SPX options are used (which they are), and the payoff is determined at the start of the box.

There are three reasons the box spread structure matters for the tax discussion of BOXX. First off, SPX options are broad based index options, which fall under IRC Section 1256. Section 1256 contracts are marked to market at year-end, and gains and losses are treated as 60% long-term/40% short-term capital gains, regardless of the holding period. Additionally, the ETF wrapper allows the fund to defer gain realization through the in-kind creation/redemption mechanism under IRC Section 852(b)(6), so realized gains are pushed out of the fund to authorized participants instead of being distributed to shareholders. Lastly, the combination produces an investor outcome where the fund earns essentially a T-bill yield, but distributes essentially nothing. The return shows up as NAV appreciation that the shareholder controls the timing of by deciding when to sell.

Rates and the Macro Picture

The fund structure of BOXX is options based, but the economics are pure short-duration Treasury exposure. The base case for 2026’s rate outlook is one cut from the Fed, and most likely in quarter 4. Core PCE remains sticky, and the Middle East conflict is keeping commodity inflation sticky as well. CME FedWatch should be checked on the publish date for current implied probabilities. With the data currently available as of mid-April 2026, FedWatch shows an approximately 85-99% probability of a hold at the April 28-29 FOMC meeting, with the first meaningful cut probability landing at the June 16-17 meeting at approx. 55-60% odds of a 25 bp reduction, albeit a coin flip. By September, cut probabilities sit near 48%. That distribution lines up with my view of one cut at most, but there is also a potential of no cut at all if Middle East commodity inflation persists into the back half of the year.

Front-end T-bill yields track where the Fed funds rate is expected to be six to twelve months out. If the Fed delivers one cut, 1-3-month bill yields drift from approx. 3.7% to approx. 3.4% over the year. If the Fed holds, yields stay near 3.7%. If the Fed has to hike because inflation reaccelerates, BOXX rolls into higher-yielding box spreads within 90 days, since the weighted average maturity of the underlying box spreads is under 90 days. There is no duration to lose, which makes ETFs like BOXX, SGOV, and BIL good ETFs for stable or higher front end rate environments.

The bear case for BOXX as a fund involves multiple Fed cuts (more than 2) in 2026, but even in that scenario, BOXX still tracks the benchmark and delivers the after-tax pickup, just with a lower absolute return level.

The Tax Math

For an investor in the top federal bracket of 37% plus 3.8% NIIT plus 5% state tax, the combined ordinary income tax rate is approximately 45.8%. SGOV after-tax return is 3.7%x(1-0.458), which is approximately 2.00%. BOXX after-tax return at long-term capital gains rates of 20% federal + 3.8% NIIT + 5% state is approximately 28.8%. The after tax return for this is 3.7%x(1-0.288), which is approximately 2.63%. 63 bps may not seem like much, but for large investors in the highest tax bracket, 63 bps can mean a fair amount of after-tax capital.

What Makes BOXX Unique

Section 1258 of the tax code recharacterizes capital gains as ordinary income when (a) substantially all of the expected return on a transaction comes from the time value of money, and (b) the transaction is a straddle under Section 1092(c) or is marketed as producing capital gains from a time-value return.

A box spread is, by construction, a synthetic loan. Its return is entirely TVM. The argument that 1258 applies is colorable. Alpha Architect's position, articulated in the prospectus tax risk section, is that the fund itself is the entity holding the box spread, not the investor, and that the ETF wrapper changes the conversion analysis. This is what enables an investor to buy and sell shares of BOXX as a standard equity security, where the taxable event is the disposition of an ETF share (a capital gain or loss), and not the settlement of a conversion transaction under Section 1258. The investor never enters into a box spread (as far as taxes are concerned). The investor simply buys a share of an ETF, and sells it later at a higher NAV.

Tradability

BOXX trades on the CBOE BZX exchange at approximately $116, with a volume of 2,284,643, translating to approximately $250 million in daily dollar volume. Bid-ask spreads run approx. 1-2 cents, which is excellent for an actively managed options-based product. The price-to-NAV spread is essentially zero. The 52-week range of $111.72 to $116.34 tells you exactly what this product is, which is a slow, steady climb that reflects accruing a box spread premium, with no volatility.

Downside Risk

In an unlikely (but possible) scenario, the Middle East conflict de-escalates, oil drops back below $70, core PCE falls toward 2.2% by Q3, and the labor market softens meaningfully (something to the tune of unemployment hitting 4.8-5.0%). The Fed responds with 100-125 bps of cuts between June and December, bringing the target range to 2.25-2.50% by year-end.

While capital loss isn’t the issue here, it is rather an opportunity cost. A fund like TLT (duration approx. 16 years) would deliver a price return of roughly 24% on the duration move alone, plus the coupon. VGIT or IEF with intermediate duration would deliver 8-12%. Even SGOV, which has the same front-end yield compression problem as BOXX, at least distributes income monthly that can be redeployed into the rallying duration trade. BOXX, by design, does not distribute. The return compounds inside the NAV, which means you cannot redeploy it without selling shares and triggering the taxable event you were trying to defer. You are locked into a depreciating yield with no optionality to rotate.

Takeaway

BOXX is a buy. My rates view of at most one Fed cut in 2026 keeps the front-end yield environment favorable for short-duration cash equivalents. The fund delivers approximately 25 bps of pre-tax convenience yield over T-bills, and approximately 60-70 bps of after-tax pickup over SGOV for high-bracket taxable investors. For taxable investors looking for cash management with a tax-deferral option, BOXX is the right ETF for today. For tax-advantaged accounts like IRAs, SGOV is cheaper and a better option.

data.title: BDCs: The Time To Buy This 14% Yielding Sector Is Now
data.description:
Introduction

As a durable income investor, I have two fundamental boxes that I seek to check before putting my precious capital to work:

  1. High dividend yield
  2. Low risk

At its core it is about finding the highest yield possible which would still fulfil the "second box" where by low risk I mean durable current income streams and sustainable NAV.

The investable universe of securities (or asset classes) that can do this is rather limited and, currently, to a certain extent not at the most attractive levels.

  • REITs (VNQ): underperforming for years, relatively low yields and likely to remain depressed due to structurally elevated long-term rates.
  • MLPs (AMLP): fairly enticing yields, but the prices are at 5-year highs due to the recent tailwinds from the war in Iran (higher oil price).
  • Infrastructure (BIP): resilient cash flows and durable growth potential but relatively unattractive yields, at least on an inflation adjusted basis.
  • Covered call ETFs (QQQI): very enticing asset class but with too heavy bias towards AI names and produces inherently volatile income streams (hence avoiding concentration here is vital).
  • Leveraged CEFs (PDO): juicy dividend yields but given the presence of external leverage that is there to magnify dividends, we have to approach this asset class with caution and avoid building up excessive exposure.

Against this backdrop I see merit in considering complementary yield-producing asset classes that could satisfy our cash flow needs and provide diversification without introducing unnecessary risk.

This is where I think BDCs can come into play.

The yields are very high, and the current discounts have created an asymmetric return profile which favors bulls (much lower downside risk than upside potential).

What's the fuss about?

It is not a secret that private credit and BDCs have become great whipping boys for the market. Before the war in Iran, private credit consumed a great deal of the headlines. Now, it has arguably fallen to a second spot.

There are multiple reasons why the market has been punishing BDCs so hard.

I wouldn't say that there was a single catalyst after which the repricing started, but instead it was an accumulation of several unfavorable data points.

This is how I'm reading the situation or finding a path for how we got here:

  1. In 2024 interest rates peaked and the market started to factor in cuts (bad for BDC earnings as most of their assets are floating).
  2. In Q1 2025 the headwinds from lower rates started to really manifest themselves into contracting NII per share results.
  3. In Q3 2025 came Tricolor and First Brands bankruptcies which led to the famous quote by Jamie Dimon: "When you see one cockroach, there are probably more". An excellent phrase for the bears to drive pessimistic headlines.
  4. In Q1 2026 Anthropic launched a product which was seemingly about to disrupt the entire software space. Given ~20% BDC exposure to SaaS or software-as-a-service names, the "SaaSpocalypse" clearly provided a backdrop for the bears to paint a calamity.
  5. Right after that the panic levels really jumped and many investors started to withdraw capital from private BDCs, which, in turn, caused many private credit managers to activate gating mechanisms (i.e., capping quarterly redemptions to 5% of NAV). Blue Owl (OWL) was the first one, but soon after that many other players "joined the party": e.g., KKR & Co (KKR), BlackRock (BLK), Ares Management (ARES) etc.

In my view, it makes sense that investors are exercising caution and assigning higher risk premiums to BDC valuations.

Yet, as it is often the case, when things go south, the market tends to overreact by letting emotions and fears dominate logic and clear facts.

In the article below I will elaborate on three main reasons why I think that this is the right time to buy BDCs.

#1: Outsized yields

Currently, BDCs are one of the highest yielding asset classes out there in which investors could lean into and find durable cash flows.

There are two drivers here.

First, the BDC core business activity is high-yield supportive.

Pre regulation BDCs are required to pay out 90% of their income to shareholders in dividends. This in combination with average loan investment yields of 10% and some degree of leverage is very accommodative for high dividend yields.

Second, BDCs are heavily discounted.

The bigger the discounts the higher yields we get from the BDC core offering. It is pure mathematics. If a $10 per share BDC trades at NAV and distributes $1 per share in dividends, then we can get a 10% yield. If the price goes down to, say, $8 per share, the BDC would trade at 20% discount to NAV and offer a higher yield of 12.5%.

The chart below encapsulates the essence of how competitive BDC yields are:

The dividend yields are very high on both absolute and relative (to the U.S. Treasury yield) level.

#2: Overblown discounts

As of today, the median BDC trades 27% below NAV.

Given that the leverage for a median BDC is 1.24x, it means that the market is pricing in a loss rate of ~12% for BDC assets (we get this by dividing 27% discount to "1+1.24"). This way we go from a P/NAV discount which shows what level of equity wipeout the market is expecting to an asset-level discount which shows what percentage of loan book the market things is about to get fully written off.

So, a 12% loss rate.

Let's put that into perspective.

1) It goes completely against what we are seeing now on the ground:

The market is pricing in ~10x jump in BDC non-accruals.

Additionally, Q4 data indicate no material uptick in non-accruals.

2) The 12% loss rate is much greater than what BDCs experienced in the COVID-19 era when the whole economy stopped (see chart above).

The loss rate that the market has baked into BDCs is comparable to levels that specific sectors experienced when they became subject to huge sector-specific defaults:

We might draw a parallel to the SaaS world.

But again, if we do the math:

  • On average BDCs have 20% of their assets in SaaS.
  • Let's assume that 30% of SaaS fail (2x bigger defaults than back in time for telcos, homebuilders and energy E&Ps; probably never going to happen).
  • Let's assume that BDCs receive absolutely nothing as senior secured lenders after doing the restructurings (also not going to happen, but on that see my next point).
  • So, if we multiply 20% by 30% we get 6% as the potential SaaS-driven loss rate for BDCs.

Namely, by conducting such an unrealistic and excessively conservative analysis we still fall short of the 12% loss rate.

3) The implied default rate is even more aggressive than 12% loss rate.

What I mean by that is that for a BDC to recognize a full loss from a single investment two things have to happen: 1) loan investment company has to default and 2) BDC has to receive $0 after the restructuring, liquidation or bankruptcy process.

And this is yet another thing that is unrealistic.

Just take a look at the historical BDC non-accrual (defaulted loan) recovery rates:

If we couple this statistic with the fact that many BDCs lend to businesses on LTVs (loan-to-value ratios) of around 50% then I think that you will agree with me that assuming some recovery rate might be a logical idea, to say the least.

So, if we take a very conservative approach and factor in a 30% recovery rate then for the current loss rate to materialize, 15.6% of median BDC assets would have to be recognized as non-accruals.

It has never happened before on a system-wide basis (not even close, not even in COVID-19).

#3: Well-performing BDCs

Obviously, some things will change as we go forward, and, most likely, in a negative direction, but I think that we have to appreciate the fact that the sector as such is in solid shape.

The public BDC non-accruals (on a cost basis) have largely remained flat at healthy levels (including Q4 data).

PIK or payment-in-kind has indeed ticked up but everything is still at historical norms:

The underlying BDC portfolio companies are also in solid shape from the credit risk perspective. For instance, BDC portfolio company average interest coverage ratio is 2.5x which means that the average company is generating EBITDA that exceeds 1.5x times what it has to pay in debt service.

The NAVs have indeed been contracting but at very small percentages which is completely logical given the presence of supplemental distributions (paying more than NII generation), precautionary markdowns and headwinds from the interest rate cuts.

In a nutshell, there is nothing indicating an approaching Armageddon (certainly, not to an extent that is priced in by the BDC bears).

Summary

I am very bullish on BDCs.

I have decided to contribute all my incremental savings into this space until the sector recovers to at least a P/NAV of 0.85x, which would require ~15% upside.

As I outlined in the piece above, there is a huge disconnect between what the market has priced in and the reality on the ground as well as historical BDC performance during severe shocks.

So, I see huge upside with the main catalyst being Q1 2026 earnings data points which will likely (in my view) indicate a continued stability.

Finally, even though I like to cherry-pick my own BDCs where I think that the risk/reward profiles are more attractive than what we can find on average in the sector, the dislocation is so huge that the two available pure-play BDC funds the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) and the Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC) could do the job as well. In other words, this is a system-wide opportunity which can be captured by ETFs that track the entire space.

data.title: Callaway Golf Company: Enjoying The Green, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Investors in Callaway Golf Company (CALY) have been enjoying some very sunny days in the green in recent periods.

Shares in the stock have gained over 100% in the past year. This includes a nearly 20% rise YTD.

The outperformance of broader markets could be viewed as rather striking, especially given CALY’s positioning in the leisure products industry and the more discretionary nature of their offerings.

As its name would imply, the company is a leading golf equipment, gear, and apparel company, whose flagship namesake, Callaway Golf, is widely known among the community.

In late 2025, CALY underwent a strategic realignment relating to its Topgolf business. Following that alignment, CALY has reaffirmed its position as a pure-play golf business, and that has apparently paid it dividends from investor sentiment.

I believe the stock is worth more attention than it's currently receiving, though I would hesitate to chase it at its highs. For existing investors, I view shares as a ‘hold’ at current pricing.

CALY Stock Key Metrics

CALY is a lightly followed stock among the community, with very little coverage from the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) community. Fellow contributor Daniel Jones has been one analyst that has continuously maintained a bullish stance on CALY, and kudos to him on the call.

The ratings summary for the SA side, accordingly, is slightly skewed due to the lack of coverage. In a similar vein, the ratings from Wall Street also show as a ‘buy,’ but the population of analysts covering the stock is quite small.

The lack of coverage is unfortunate, in my view, especially given the run-up in the stock price, which I view as well warranted.

At current pricing, CALY trades at a forward of nearly 30x earnings. That’s rich, yes, but CALY does have a dominant position in the golf equipment and apparel market, with limited competition.

CALY also appears richly valued through other metrics as well, such as its current enterprise value (“EV”) to EBITDA and sales, each of which stands at about 18x and 1.6x, respectively.

Some would also argue against the higher multiple due to CALY’s growth and earnings performance. In its most recent filing, for example, it recorded negative YOY revenue change and missed on earnings estimates.

That doesn’t exactly inspire much confidence in the company, in my view.

Nonetheless, I do believe the outlook ahead is positive and that CALY does have some favorable demand drivers that could help propel revenues higher in later reporting periods.

What Is The Outlook For CALY Stock?

Coming out of Q4, the outlook for CALY looks noticeably different than it did just a year ago, and that’s primarily due to the reset button pushed by the management team.

With the sale of Jack Wolfskin and the 60% stake in Topgolf, CALY views itself as once again a pure-play golf company with a net cash balance sheet, no Topgolf debt exposure, and a much cleaner operating story for investors to follow.

The repositioning, which occurred in late 2025, is likely to account for the current upward movement in the share price.

Just as important, the demand factors for golf in general seem extremely healthy. According to the National Golf Foundation, for example, rounds played continue to set records, participation is broadening across women, youth, and people of color, and off-course engagement is feeding more interest back into on-course play.

In my view, that’s a favorable demand environment for a company with top-tier share positions in clubs, balls, and putters globally.

That said, President and CEO, Oliver Brewer, was still very upfront on the Q4 earnings call that 2026 would be a transition year.

Revenue, for one, is expected to dip slightly as CALY deliberately pulls back from lower-margin channels, extends product life cycles, and invests more heavily into fitting and premium positioning. Add in another +$40M in tariff pressure, and near-term earnings optics also look weak.

Longer term, however, I believe these moves should improve margins, free cash flow, and market share.

What To Watch When CALY Reports Q1 Results?

Looking ahead, CALY guided for roughly 3% YOY revenue growth and solid EBITDA, even while absorbing an incremental +$24M in tariff expense. If CALY can hit those targets while protecting margins, that will say a lot about how well its pricing power and product mix are holding up early in the year.

I’ll be paying particularly close attention to gross margin commentary. Last year, the golf equipment segment actually expanded margins before tariffs. To me, this shows the internal initiatives are working.

If we see further progress here, it would likely support the idea that this is becoming a more profitable business even if revenue growth slows.

New product reception will also be key. Early feedback from retail and “green grass” partners on the Quantum woods, irons, and Odyssey putters sounded strong. Evidence of healthy sell-through as we enter the peak spring season would be encouraging from my lens.

Lastly, listen for commentary on channel rationalization and fitting investments. These are the deliberate changes that may pressure near-term sales but are central to CALY's long-term margin expansion story.

Is CALY Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

After a run of more than 100% over the past year, I think it’s fair to say much of the good news is already reflected in the share price.

In my view, CALY today is a very different company than it was 12 months ago. The exits from Jack Wolfskin and Topgolf have simplified the business, cleaned up the balance sheet, and returned the company to its roots as a focused golf equipment and apparel leader.

That reset, combined with a very healthy demand environment for the sport, gives me confidence that the long-term direction of the business is pointed the right way.

However, 2026 is clearly shaping up to be a reset year operationally. Based on the Q4 call, it was clear to me that the management team is intentionally sacrificing some near-term revenue to improve margins, product mix, and channel quality.

Add on tariff pressure and softer consumer confidence, and the earnings picture in the short run is not especially compelling for a stock trading around 30x forward earnings.

None of this makes CALY a ‘sell,’ in my view. The brands remain strong, the market position is enviable, and the balance sheet is now a real strength.

But after such a sharp move higher, this looks like a comfortable 'hold' for existing investors. And for new investors, I'd rather wait for a better entry point than chase at the highs.

data.title: PPG Industries: Price Hikes Will Help Stabilize Margins
data.description:
It’s been around a year since my first article on PPG Industries, Inc. (PPG), so there is no better time to look at the company’s FY25 performance and what is still in store for 2026.

Briefly on Performance

For the first half of 2025, the company struggled to grow its top line. FX headwinds, as well as divestitures, affected the company’s performance throughout the first two quarters. From the third quarter onwards, the previous divestitures were out of the way, with organic sales rising by over 2%, pushed higher by stronger aerospace, protective, and marine demand. The last quarter of the year was particularly good, with 5% growth y/y, 3% organic growth, with higher selling prices and sales volumes across all regions.

Over the last year, the company’s profitability took a dip, unfortunately. Global architectural coatings was the biggest drag early in the year, with Q1 sales down due to FX and lower volumes, with EBITDA margins falling to 16.8% from 19.9%. Another drag in Q1 was the Industrial coatings segment, with EBITDA decreasing by 90bps. The segment that slightly helped offset the weakness was the Performance coating segment, which not only maintained its EBITDA, but even increased it by 20bps. Q2 showed very similar performance across the segments.

It’s only in Q4 that we see a marked improvement once again in the two underperforming segments, but nothing comes easy, as the Performance Coatings segment saw a decent dip in profitability. Segment’s EBITDA went from 23.1% to 21.1%. Automotive was the culprit behind the underperformance. Overall, FY25 saw around 80bps decrease in profitability.

Looking at other key profitability metrics, we can see that, with the decent boost in the bottom line, PPG’s efficiency has vastly improved as well, as evidenced by an increase across all three metrics below. The management is much more efficient at utilizing the company’s assets, as well as shareholder capital. ROTC is close to my minimum of 10%, so if the momentum continues, PPG will be quite attractive.

Going over the company’s financial position, PPG finished the year with over $2.1B in cash and equivalents against $6.6B in long-term debt, which is quite a lot. But is it worrisome? I don’t think so. The company’s interest coverage ratio for the year was 9x, which is well above what most analysts think is healthy (2x) and also higher than what I am looking for, which is 5x.

Also, the company’s cash flow has improved over the last year as well. Cash from operations was close to $2B, with free cash flow at around $1.16B vs. $669M last year. The company is a cash machine even as profitability declined, and top-line performance was quite lackluster.

Preliminary Results

PPG went ahead and pre-announced its first-quarter EPS numbers that increased around 6% over last year’s Q1 to $1.83. I would say the real reason for pre-announcing is that they had big news to share. PPG said that they are looking to increase prices by up to 20% across all product lines due to increased cost pressures and supply constraints.

What this means for the company.

That is quite a decent price hike. I wouldn’t say this tool is looking to increase margins and profitability for the sake of it. We saw that the company’s profitability took a hit in 2025, so I view it as more of a defensive and corrective tool. The company is trying to push through higher costs, protect supply continuity, and restore its margins after a disappointing year like 2025, when its operating leverage weakened.

I doubt a 20% increase is going to flow through to the bottom line. There will be some customer pushback, timing lags, and contracts in place before the hike was introduced, so the increase will flow through gradually.

Additionally, the company is still dealing with soft industrial and European demand, and the expectation of these segments is that they will remain challenged a little while longer. It makes sense to hike prices; the company has the pricing power and better end-market exposure. For instance, aerospace is one positive I can see that will help its operations continue to grow.

With the release of Q1 ’26 numbers looming on April 28, I will be looking out for more details on how the operations will develop with the price hikes in place for the rest of 2026. Should we expect a guidance adjustment to the upside? I wouldn’t rule it out. In the pre-announcement, the company said that it expects second-quarter organic sales and adjusted earnings to be flat to low single-digit percentage growth, but I would like to hear about Q3 and Q4, as that is where we are going to get most of the flow-through of the price hikes.

In terms of other important growth factors, I am going to look for how the company’s performance coatings come in for Q1, after a marked decline in Q4. Also, I will be looking out for the strength in the other two segments to persist into Q1 and not revert to declines as we saw in Q1 and Q2 last year. It’s a possibility that they will.

As I mentioned earlier, European industrial demand and broader industrial markets have been a drag recently, so I would like to hear some comments on these areas to gauge what we should expect going forward. They already said that they expect them to remain subdued, but any indication of recovery is welcome.

Aerospace has been the one decent compounder and a savior for the company over the last while, so I would like to see this end market remain resilient going forward. High utilization, specification, and recurring maintenance demand are what make aerospace so attractive. The opportunity comes from two places: new aircraft production and maintenance/repaint demand. Airline traffic is poised for growth in 2026, and with last year’s announcement of a $380m aerospace coatings and sealants facility, the demand is there, as the company’s backlog in the segment continues to grow.

Should you buy PPG?

I am looking to sit this one out, given the PPG share price performance over the last year. I don’t see much growth yet, but I will be looking out for what kind of effect the price hike is going to have in the long run. Usually, when many manufacturers raise prices, they hardly drop them again, even as costs decline, unless the competition begins to cut and undermine them. So, I will monitor the margin progress going forward, but I don’t think there is an opportunity for me just yet.

data.title: Figma: Is It Finally Time? Partially (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Figma, Inc. (FIG) shares have collapsed 83% in less than a year after a post-IPO bounce that took the company's valuation to unprecedented overvaluation territory.

I warned investors that the fall will be steep and that it's important to wait patiently for a real, fundamentally sound valuation, as even after the initial 70% fall, I found the stock to be extremely overvalued.

As the stock has fallen another 47% since, investors are once again asking, is it finally time? And my answer is, simply, no. Well, partially.

Figma's Growth Shows All Arrows Point Towards The Right Direction

Figma continues to grow at a rapid pace, exceeding consensus estimates on both revenue and non-GAAP EPS for the third consecutive quarter as a public company.

Revenue in Q4 rose 40% to a record $304 million, reflecting a nice 2-point acceleration from 38% growth in Q3.

Net dollar retention rate also expanded to 136%, the highest level of the past two years.

Moreover, Figma started disclosing its gross retention rate, which is a far more important metric to the “terminal value” discussion, as it shows the actual churn of customers (unlike net dollar retention, where one customer's expansion offsets another customer's churn). Of note, the 97% mark is quite strong and appears to be in line with the previous seven quarters.

Another angle that shows strong growth is in the number of significant paying customers, with the $10,000+ cohort expanding by 951 customers and the $100,000 group expanding by 143. If you want to nitpick, this is somewhat of a deceleration, but not a meaningful one, and it depends on which quarter you compare it to.

Looking ahead, Figma guided for 38% growth in Q1-26 and 30% growth in FY26. While this reflects a slowdown from the 41% level in 2025, this is still an impressive level, and it came above consensus expectations.

Except For The Most Crucial Part - Profits

I think we can say that Figma's growth remains exceptional and that, as far as the numbers show, there are no AI existential headwinds to speak of. To me, I'm much more concerned because of something else, and that's profits.

Non-GAAP gross margins continue to drop as Figma's AI offerings, which are margin-dilutive, gain traction. Management said they are consistently reducing their cost-to-serve per user, but the difference in margin between an AI offering and a legacy software solution is likely meaningful. In other words, there's a long way to go before we find out the representative margin rate.

While AI is supposed to be a productivity enhancer, so far, what we're seeing is that for Figma, it's mainly a top-line driver but a net negative on productivity and margins. Gross margins dropped 6 points Y/Y, and non-GAAP operating margins dropped 12 points, with management citing investments in people, infrastructure, and systems.

For 2026, they expect to deliver $105 million of non-GAAP operating income at the midpoint, which would reflect 7.7% margins and another step lower in margins relative to the 12% achieved in 2026.

All that margin talk and I didn't mention perhaps the most critical part: we are, of course, talking about non-GAAP margins.

In Figma's case, those non-GAAP adjustments primarily include stock-based compensation, at a jaw-dropping amount, to be frank, and $1.3 billion, to be exact. If we assume the same level of SBC in 2026, we're talking about a $1.2 billion operating loss instead of a $105 million profit. And, if we assume the run rate of Q4, we're talking about a $700 million loss.

Either way, it's hard to make the case that Figma is some type of value play here, after falling 83%.

Valuation Remains Stretched Based On Fundamentals

Because Figma is far from generating real profits or cash flows, we can't really evaluate it based on traditional fundamentals.

One way to look at it is adjusted EPS, which puts Figma at 87 times forward earnings and 71 times next year's estimates.

This is far above other software names, but Figma is growing two or three times as fast as some of them, so based on a PEG ratio, the gap isn't as high, still very high though.

Another way to look at valuation is based on revenues, where Figma trades at 6.6 times forward sales:

This also places them far above most software names, but again, not relative to the expected growth.

Once a real fundamental valuation is no longer an option, there's not much to get excited about here in my view, but there is a legitimate case to be made that Figma's stock is getting closer to reasonable levels. Still not all the way there, though.

Conclusion

Even after the 83% fall, Figma is objectively far from being attractively valued based on fundamentals. Meanwhile, it's hard to take a leap of faith and bet on the company's long-term profit potential when margins keep contracting and AI creates significant uncertainties.

That said, gross retention is exceptionally strong, growth-adjusted non-GAAP multiples are at reasonable levels, and Figma has shown it can deliver frontier AI-based solutions to its customers.

At $20 per share, I no longer view Figma as a 'Sell,' but it's still not attractive to me. As such, I upgrade the stock to 'Hold.'
